data.content:
Use `dotnet watch test` — it re-runs tests automatically whenever you save a file. To focus on just the failing test:

```
dotnet watch test --filter "RemoveStock_ExceedsAvailable_ThrowsException"
```

Let me start it for you so you can see it in action:

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch for the failing test

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Start dotnet watch for the failing test

data.reasoningText:
The user wants to set up hot reload for their test project. Since they're using .NET 10 with Microsoft.Testing.Platform (as indicated by the global.json), they can use `dotnet test --hotreload` or `dotnet watch test`.

Let me check what's available. With .NET 10 and the Microsoft Testing Platform, `dotnet watch` should work. Let me suggest using `dotnet watch test` which will re-run tests automatically when files change.

Actually, with the newer Microsoft Testing Platform in .NET 10, there might be a `--hotreload` flag. But the standard approach is `dotnet watch test`.


I'll set this up with `dotnet watch test` and add a filter to focus on the specific failing test.

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch for the failing test

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-860d72217e1a4beabb4165476d48a56a && dotnet watch test --filter \"RemoveStock_ExceedsAvailable_ThrowsException\" 2>&1", "description": "Start dotnet watch for the failing test", "mode": "async", "shellId": "dotnet-watch"}

data.reasoningText:
The issue is that the project is using VSTest runner but global.json specifies Microsoft.Testing.Platform. I need to configure the project to use Microsoft.Testing.Platform. Let me fix the project file first, then set up the watch.

Actually, let me add the required property to the csproj to enable the Microsoft Testing Platform runner.

data.content: The project needs to opt in to the Microsoft Testing Platform runner. Let me fix the `.csproj` and restart:
